title: SEGMENT 2 — Wild adenovirus: what it is and how it makes you sick

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Wild adenovirus: what it is and how it makes you sick
Let's start, the way we always should, with the natural history.
What is adenovirus actually doing out in the world, before we got our hands on it?
Adenovirus is a family of viruses — the Adenoviridae — that infect vertebrates broadly.
Birds, fish, reptiles, mammals — every major vertebrate lineage has its own adenoviruses.
The ones that infect humans are called the human adenoviruses, or HAdV, and there are something like eighty-eight distinct types known so far, organized into seven species labeled A through G.
Each type has a different surface protein profile, a slightly different tissue preference, a slightly different disease pattern.
Most of them cause mild illness.
A few can be nastier, particularly in babies and in immunocompromised people.
The bread-and-butter adenoviral diseases are respiratory.
Sore throat, runny nose, cough, fever — a typical adenoviral upper respiratory infection is indistinguishable from any other common cold.
In children especially, adenovirus is a major cause of pharyngitis.
It can also cause pinkeye — viral conjunctivitis — and outbreaks of pinkeye in schools and military barracks are often traceable to a specific adenoviral type.
There's a type, Ad14, that has caused some genuinely dangerous pneumonia outbreaks, particularly in military trainees in the US in the 2000s.
There's a cluster of types that cause gastroenteritis — adenoviral diarrhea, usually in infants.
And there are types that cause hemorrhagic cystitis in bone marrow transplant patients.
The list goes on.
But for the majority of healthy adults, an adenoviral infection is unmemorable.
You feel lousy for a few days, your immune system clears it, you make antibodies against the viral capsid proteins, and you go on with your life.

Those antibodies stay with you — and this is going to matter enormously when we get to vaccines — because pre-existing immunity to common adenoviral types is the central problem of using those types as vectors.
Let me put a number on the pre-existing immunity issue, because it shocks people the first time they hear it.
The most-studied adenoviral type, the one most early vectors were built on, is adenovirus type 5 — Ad5.
It's a perfectly ordinary respiratory adenovirus.
In adult populations in the United States and Europe, somewhere between forty and seventy percent of people have detectable neutralizing antibodies against Ad5.
In some sub-Saharan African populations, the prevalence is over ninety percent.

That means if you take an Ad5-based vector — a souped-up, payload-loaded version of Ad5 — and infuse it into a random adult patient, there's a very good chance their immune system already has antibodies waiting to neutralize the virus before it does anything useful.
The vector gets chewed up at the front door.
We'll come back to this in Segment 11; for now just lock in the number — most adults have already met Ad5.
Now, what does wild adenovirus actually do when it infects a cell?
Here's the cycle, briefly.
The virion — that's the technical term for a single virus particle — lands on a cell's surface.
It binds, via a long protruding protein called the fiber, to a specific receptor on the cell membrane.
For most adenoviral types, including Ad5, that receptor is called CAR — coxsackie and adenovirus receptor.
CAR is a cell-cell adhesion molecule, normally involved in holding epithelial cells together in tight junctions.
Adenovirus hijacked it as an entry door.

Once the fiber is bound to CAR, a second viral protein at the corners of the capsid — the penton base — engages integrins on the cell surface, and the virus gets pulled inside through a process called clathrin-mediated endocytosis.
Imagine the cell forming a little pocket around the virus and pinching it off into an internal vesicle.

Inside the cell, the virus escapes from that vesicle into the cytoplasm — a clever bit of molecular acrobatics involving the partial uncoating of the capsid — and then trundles along microtubules toward the nucleus.
At the nuclear pore, the virus essentially injects its DNA genome through the pore into the nucleus.
The capsid stays outside; the DNA goes in.

Once inside the nucleus, the viral genome stays as an episome — a free-floating piece of DNA, not integrated into the host chromosome — and starts using the cell's transcription and translation machinery to make viral proteins.
New capsids are assembled, new genomes are packaged, and eventually the cell lyses and thousands of new virions are released.
That whole cycle takes about a day and a half in a tissue culture cell.
It's fast.
It's efficient.
And it leaves behind a host cell that has dedicated essentially all its machinery to making virus.
The reason I'm walking you through this cycle in detail is that every step is a design choice we can intervene on when we engineer the virus into a vector.
We can change the fiber to change what cells the virus binds to.
We can delete the early viral genes that hijack the host machinery, to make the virus crippled and unable to replicate.
We can keep the part that gets the genome into the nucleus — the entry machinery — and gut everything else.
The wild virus is a complete machine.
The engineered vector is the same machine with most of the working parts taken out and replaced with cargo.
But before we start gutting, we need to look at the architecture of the virion itself, because that's the part we don't usually mess with.
The capsid is what the immune system sees.
The capsid is what binds the receptor.
The capsid is, in a sense, the truck's chassis — and the chassis matters a lot.
